content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance/Administration      Executive/Legal      Enforcement      Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660                           322-5901            322-6441            322-6444

May 19, 1978

Mr. R. W. Johnson, P.E.
Director, San Lorenzo Valley
County Water District
Post Office Box H
Boulder Creek, California  95006

Dear Mr. Johnson:

     This letter is in response to your correspondence
dated May 7, 1978, in which you requested advice concern-
ing the duties of Director Peterson, Commission regulation
2 Cal.Adm. Code §18329, which outlines Commission advice
procedure, provides that the staff shall provide advice to
officials regarding their duties under the Political Reform
Act.  However, it has been the Commission's long-standing
policy to refrain from advising third persons with respect
to the duties of public officials except in the context of
a complaint.

     The purpose behind this policy is to provide a maximum
degree of fairness to both the public and the public offi-
cial.  The Commission recognized that third persons request-
ing advice regarding the activities of public officials
frequently are not in the best position to know all of the
facts surrounding the issue in question.  Therefore, the
Commission concluded that it would be inappropriate to
issue advice under circumstances in which the public offi-
cial had not had an opportunity to set forth his view of
the matter.

     Unfortunately, it is not feasible for the Commission
to conduct a full-scale investigation in every case surround-
ing a request for advice.  Therefore, the Commission limits
its investigative power to situations involving potential
violations of the Act.  In such cases, the Commission has
adopted extensive procedures to protect the rights of the
person being investigated.

R.W. Johnson, P.E.
May 19, 1978
Page Two


     The only exception to this general rule involves
questions from third parties which do not involve an
interpretation of facts. This was the case with respect
to your previous letter regarding Director Gec.

     In light of the above discussion, I am sure you can
appreciate the problems that we would encounter if we
issued advice to third persons regarding the duties of
public officials. I must, therefore, refrain from answer-
ing those questions regarding Director Peterson's activities
which involve an interpretation of facts. However, since
Director Peterson has written a letter to the Commission
requesting guidance on many of the same issues outlined
in your letter, I will provide you with a copy of our
response to Director Peterson when it is completed.

     With respect to questions eight and ten, which do not
involve an interpretation of fact, I hope the following
informal advice is helpful. In question eight you asked
whether Mr. Amrhein is precluded from advising the District
in such a way so as to affect the amount of fees which he
receives in his capacity as the water district's attorney.
Government Code §82030(b)(2) provides that salary or reim-
bursement expenses from a local government agency are
not "income" within the meaning of the Political Reform
Act. Since Mr. Amrhein's fees from the District are in
the nature of salary, we do not think that such fees
constitute income within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act. Therefore, Mr. Amrhein would not be disquali-
fied, by any provisions of the Political Reform Act,
from issuing advice to the Board which may result in more
or less fees for him in his capacity as attorney for the
District.

     In question nine, you asked several questions concerning
the application of §91000. By way of background, §91000 pro-
vides that the District Attorney is the criminal prosecutor
for violations of the Political Reform Act by members of
local government agencies. This means that the District
Attorney has the power to bring criminal lawsuits against
members of local government agencies for violations of
the Political Reform Act. It does not authorize or require
the District Attorney to issue opinions or advice interpret-
ing provisions of the Act. Therefore, the Fair Political

R. W. Johnson, P.E.
May 19, 1978
Page Three


Practices Commission does not intend to order the District
Attorney to issue advice to the District interpreting
provisions of the Act. However, the Commission will be
happy to assist the District in any way it can in resolving
questions arising under the Act.

If I may be of further assistance, please do not hesitate
to contact me.

Sincerely,


Ted Prim, Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division


TP:gb

sections.question_synthetic: The requester asked whether the FPPC could provide advice regarding Director Peterson's duties under the Political Reform Act, and also posed specific questions about an attorney's ability to advise on matters affecting his fees and the District Attorney's role in issuing interpretive opinions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declines to advise third parties on the duties of public officials except in complaint contexts to ensure fairness, but will share its response to Director Peterson's own request. For the specific non-fact-based questions: an attorney's fees from a local agency are not 'income' under the Political Reform Act, so the attorney is not disqualified from advising on matters affecting his fees; and the District Attorney has criminal prosecution authority but is not required to issue interpretive opinions on the Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers present; question and conclusion content extracted from letter body. Document discusses FPPC policy on third-party advice requests and provides selective guidance on questions 8, 9, and 10 while declining to answer fact-intensive questions about Director Peterson.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The requester asked whether the FPPC could provide advice regarding Director Peterson's duties under the Political Reform Act, and also posed specific questions about an attorney's ability to advise on matters affecting his fees and the District Attorney's role in issuing interpretive opinions.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC declines to advise third parties on the duties of public officials except in complaint contexts to ensure fairness, but will share its response to Director Peterson's own request. For the specific non-fact-based questions: an attorney's fees from a local agency are not 'income' under the Political Reform Act, so the attorney is not disqualified from advising on matters affecting his fees; and the District Attorney has criminal prosecution authority but is not required to issue interpretive opinions on the Act.

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC response to a third party's request for advice regarding Director Peterson's duties under the Political Reform Act. The Commission declines to advise third parties on public officials' conduct except in complaints, but provides informal guidance on two specific non-factual questions regarding attorney conflicts of interest and the District Attorney's role.
